title: ps: do not sync container
body:
Do not sync containers with the runtime and the database when listing containers.  It turns out to be extremely expensive and unnecessary.

The sync was needed since listing all containers from the database did not populate their state.  Doing that, however, is much faster since we already have a connection to the database.

This change makes listing 200 containers 2 times faster than before.

[NO NEW TESTS NEEDED]

Signed-off-by: Valentin Rothberg <vrothberg@redhat.com>

@baude @mheon PTAL, this in combination with yesterday's PR makes listing 200 containers 3 times faster.  I think there is still something left to squeeze.

@containers/podman-maintainers PTAL

[APPROVALNOTIFIER] This PR is **APPROVED** This pull-request has been approved by: *vrothberg* The full list of commands accepted by this bot can be found [here](https://go.k8s.io/bot-commands?repo=containers%2Fpodman). The pull request process is described [here](https://git.k8s.io/community/contributors/guide/owners.md#the-code-review-process)
Needs approval from an approver in each of these files: - ~~[OWNERS](https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/main/OWNERS)~~ [vrothberg] Approvers can indicate their approval by writing `/approve` in a comment Approvers can cancel approval by writing `/approve cancel` in a comment

> In short reading the state without holding the container lock means we are never using an accurate state. It may not be a big deal for a command like ps because by the time we unlock and print the output the state could have already be changed anyway but I still think we should never use the state without holding the container lock regardless.

The crux of the matter is that whatever state we return in `ps` is potentially outdated.  Once we release the lock and return, the data is out of date.

It's certainly an optimization for `ps` only but other commands or call paths won't be affected as they reload the state during `sync`.

Well I just say this because I remember I tried this in https://github.com/containers/podman/pull/11689 and @mheon was against it, https://github.com/containers/podman/commit/1db70cce344724e091635bc8c6fa6c207450df68 was linked.


> It's certainly an optimization for ps only but other commands or call paths won't be affected as they reload the state during sync.

Other code paths are impacted as they now are slower because of the extra state unmarshal on the first retrieval, so that should be tested as well. I am not saying this is measurable but there are downsides with that approach.
It can also easier lead to developers forgetting the `syncContainer()` call in critical sections because it already looks like they have a valid state.

> Well I just say this because I remember I tried this in https://github.com/containers/podman/pull/11689 and @mheon was against it, https://github.com/containers/podman/commit/1db70cce344724e091635bc8c6fa6c207450df68 was linked.

Thanks for checking!  I chatted with @mheon yesterday (looked at https://github.com/containers/podman/commit/1db70cce344724e091635bc8c6fa6c207450df68) and he was on board.

> Other code paths are impacted as they now are slower because of the extra state unmarshal on the first retrieval, so that should be tested as well.

I did but couldn't measure an impact on other commands.  Those are mostly linked to `--all`.  I have one local change that parallelizes loading each container which squeezes ~10-20 percent of performance.  But I need more testing on that.

The remote job keeps timing out.  It may be a real issue. But I'll be buried in meetings until the end of the day.

In case it continues to fail, I'll take a look tomorrow morning.

I don't like this, but the primary thing this actually broke (retrieving network namespaces from disk) should be addressed by the recent patches @Luap99 got in. It's still a bit of a hack, but one of the few viable ways to substantially reduce expensive DB traffic.

Added a 2nd commit (for easier reviewing) making loading the states optional.
@Luap99 @mheon PTanotherL

@vrothberg: new pull request created: #17248
